engagement.justification: Directly takes up how relationship-role changes obligation, listing roles and grounds (closeness, shared history, consequences), though it drifts into a vaguer middle-point discussion and a dangling final sentence.

coherence.justification: Mostly readable with a structured 'one way/other way/third way' frame, but the ordering is muddled and it ends mid-thought ('think about being even').

substance.justification: Identifies relevant distinctions (role-based duty, shared history, future consequences) but does not push any into a sharp principle about why identity changes obligation.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF carries the relational reasoning fairly naturally ('the one who gives work and money'), only mildly clunky.

engagement.justification: Does not address the chemical-element biconditional at all; talks vaguely about 'ways' and 'ends' with no connection to the definition.

coherence.justification: Self-contradictory and fragmented; 'two ways of making the way have nothing to do with the way of making the part of the way' is incoherent.

substance.justification: No case, distinction, or counterexample — only empty restatement-like word salad.

expressive_adequacy.justification: The restriction yields nonsense; no argument survives the phrasing.

engagement.justification: Two disconnected sentences about 'feeling' and 'signs of truth' that never touch the definition of courage.

coherence.justification: Each short sentence is locally grammatical but they do not connect into any argument.

substance.justification: No counterexample to the courage definition; no distinction developed.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Words are simple but the fragment is too thin to judge as adequate; nothing is expressed.

engagement.justification: Squarely engages how the mind builds a whole percept from fragmentary sensory pieces, the central ask.

coherence.justification: Clear first/second/third structure that follows logically; only minor slips like a misfired 'eating that sweet thing' example.

substance.justification: Develops the idea with prior-knowledge inference and a concrete tree/fruit example, going beyond restatement, though it stops short of a non-obvious distinction.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF feels native here — 'tiny points that tell us about the shape' conveys sense-data cleanly.

engagement.justification: Gestures at a conditional about being unsure/showing one is wrong but never engages a defeasible-conditional task on its own terms.

coherence.justification: Question-chained and disjointed; references to 'one who talks about god' and 'less sad than him' do not connect.

substance.justification: Hints at defeasibility (being sure vs being shown wrong) but develops nothing.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Clunky and only partly recoverable; the restriction muddies an already thin point.

engagement.justification: Prompt is a modus-ponens weather-planning conditional, but the response is a doctrine-of-the-mean essay on courage/fear — entirely off-topic.

coherence.justification: The mean-between-extremes essay is internally readable for a stretch, with some garbled sentences ('If us go too far'), and it trails off.

substance.justification: Develops the mean idea somewhat but it is irrelevant to the asked conditional; no on-topic substance.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF mostly carries the off-topic essay but with clunky breakdowns ('area feel the same to us').

engagement.justification: Directly addresses why full disclosure is not always best, the central ask.

coherence.justification: Numbered reasons follow coherently and a synthesis pulls them together; trails off at the end and a couple of points are fuzzy.

substance.justification: Offers several genuine considerations (harm to the unprepared, distortion by others, loss of one's own edge) and a decision rule, going well beyond truism.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF reads naturally throughout; the restriction does not impede the multi-point argument.

engagement.justification: Prompt is a denying-the-antecedent child-rearing conditional; the two-sentence reply about 'strong people who do what' is unrelated and empty.

coherence.justification: Fragmented and meaningless ('right end for those who do what').

substance.justification: No content addressing the inference pattern.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Phrasing collapses into nonsense.

engagement.justification: Engages utilitarian 'most good' directly, building up what it means and how to apply it.

substance.justification: Develops the consequentialist criterion with a food-sharing case and a sacrifice case, and notes the focus on outcomes vs acts.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF carries the moral-theory content naturally ('the whole part of good in the world is higher').

engagement.justification: Glances at the trust definition ('if person means how not doing these things changes what is true') but does not actually engage it.

coherence.justification: A single garbled run-on that does not resolve into a claim.

substance.justification: No counterexample or distinction about trust.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Barely recoverable; the restriction leaves only fragments.

substance.justification: No relevant content.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Simple words but the passage is incoherent, so adequacy cannot be credited.

engagement.justification: Drifts from the whole/parts (mereology) prompt into an epistemology essay on knowledge vs true belief; touches 'parts/wholes' only at the opening before missing the actual ask.

engagement.justification: Prompt asks for a counterexample about a bounded region/space; the reply is a JTB/broken-clock epistemology essay missing the ask.

coherence.justification: The epistemology essay is partly coherent but riddled with broken sentences ('let us has a first part', 'sights take us a reason').

substance.justification: Develops the broken-clock Gettier idea, a relevant epistemic case, but unrelated to the bounded-space definition.

engagement.justification: Touches how a group agrees on rules good for all, then pivots to majority-vs-individual conflict without resolving the central ask.

engagement.justification: Directly engages how repeated good actions form a good character (habituation).

substance.justification: Develops the habituation thesis with the walking-path analogy and the house-of-pieces image — a genuine, fitting development.

expressive_adequacy.justification: UGF mostly native with the path metaphor; minor breakdowns keep it from full marks.

engagement.justification: Engages the 'most good' principle directly and even raises its tensions.

coherence.justification: Largely coherent; one over-long sentence near the end blurs but the argument holds.

substance.justification: Pushes beyond statement: weighs amount of good against kind of act, raises absolute prohibitions and epistemic limits on foreseeing consequences — a genuine, non-obvious development.

coherence.justification: Disjointed; the two sentences do not connect.

substance.justification: No counterexample about climate; only a faint epistemic gesture.

expressive_adequacy.justification: Barely recoverable fragment.
